title: Bioinformatics
text:
What I am curious about

Can artificial intelligence help uncover ecological patterns and microbial interactions that remain hidden within complex environmental sequencing datasets?
Can advances in AI and bioinformatics accelerate the discovery of microbial functions that support resilient ecosystems?

title: Sustainable Agriculture
text:
Research Questions

How do fermentation-derived microbial biofertilisers influence plant growth, health and productivity?
Which microbial taxa and functional traits promote resilient plant–microbiome interactions under different biofertiliser and substrate type?
How do these microbes break into the mineral matrix to release these useful elements needed for plant growth and development?

title: Sustainable Agriculture
text:
Deciphering Plant–Microbiome Interactions
My current research will investigate how microbial biofertilisers influence crop performance and soil microbial communities under uncontrolled experimental conditions in the field. Complementary greenhouse experiments will also be done later to investigate similar effects under controlled conditions.
By integrating experimental biology with plant performance measurements with molecular sequencing and microbial community analyses, this research aims to identify how beneficial microorganisms contribute to sustainable agricultural production and improved crop resilience.
Fieldwork for this project will be carried out in Hawai’i and last until March/April next year. Stay tuned in this space to know more!

title: Outreach Philosophy
text:
I believe science should be accessible to everyone. Whether speaking to school children, university students, tourists or fellow researchers, my goal is always the same—to make scientific discoveries understandable, engaging and relevant.
The ability to communicate research clearly is just as important as conducting it.

title: Outreach Philosophy
text:
Polar Bears International (2026)



Polar Bears International glasshouse featuring Annie


Following my PhD, I worked as a Science Communicator with Polar Bears International in Svalbard. During the summer tourist season, I engaged with visitors arriving on expedition cruise ships, discussing Arctic ecosystems, polar bear ecology, sea ice loss and the impacts of climate change on the rapidly changing Arctic environment.
This role strengthened my passion for communicating complex scientific concepts to diverse audiences and demonstrated the importance of connecting scientific research with public understanding.

title: Outreach Philosophy
text:
Open Day Events at UNIS



Mirror Mirror on the wall, whos the tallest of them all


Throughout my PhD, I helped organize and deliver several public outreach events at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS). Together with colleagues, we designed interactive, hands-on activities that introduced children and families to Arctic science, climate change and microbial life through fun and engaging experiments.
These events aimed to spark curiosity about the natural world while encouraging the next generation of scientists. Although the picture above doesnt show the work we did that day, but is still a little sneak peak into the fieldwork life of a marine biologist, that we made sure to show the students.

title: Outreach Philosophy
text:
Pint of Science



Blurry picture, but happy faces. Featuring my PhD ride-or-dies from UNIS, Svalbard! ❤️


As part of The Nansen Legacy, one of Norway’s largest Arctic research initiatives, I helped organise the Pint of Science, an international science communication festival that brings researchers and the public together in informal settings. Through this event, I had the opportunity to share my PhD research on Arctic microbial ecology with audiences outside academia and discuss why microscopic organisms are fundamental to understanding a rapidly changing Arctic.
👉 The Nansen Legacy – PhD Fellows

title: About
text:
I’ve always been fascinated by the invisible world. The fact that microscopic organisms, unseen to the naked eye, can shape entire ecosystems continues to inspire the questions I ask as a scientist.
My research sits at the intersection of molecular ecology, microbial ecology, bioinformatics and environmental sequencing, where I use DNA-based approaches to understand how microbial communities respond to environmental change.
During my PhD at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS), I spent several years studying microbial eukaryote communities across Arctic fjords using one of the longest environmental DNA time series collected in the European Arctic. That work revealed how seasonal light cycles, hydrography and changing environmental conditions shape microbial biodiversity in one of Earth’s most rapidly changing ecosystems.
Today, my research is expanding beyond marine systems into plant microbiomes, sustainable agriculture and ultimately space biology, where I hope to understand how beneficial microbial communities can help support resilient ecosystems both on Earth and during future long-duration space missions.

title: Successfully Defended My PhD
text:
After five and a half years of research, fieldwork, sequencing, coding and writing, I successfully defended my PhD in Arctic microbial ecology at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS) and University of Agder (UiA).
Interested in learning more about the science behind my PhD?
 🧬 Explore My PhD Research 
Beyond the science, this PhD completely shaped who I am. It challenged me, humbled me, introduced me to lifelong friends and mentors, and gave me the privilege of calling one of the most extraordinary places on Earth home for over five years.

title: Research
text:
My research spans microbial ecology across extreme environments, combining molecular biology, environmental DNA, bioinformatics and ecological theory to understand how microbial communities respond to environmental change.





Understanding how microbial communities respond to seasonality and environmental change across Arctic fjords using long-term environmental DNA datasets.

title: Teaching & Mentorship
text:
During my PhD at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS), and following the completion of my doctorate, I contributed to teaching through lectures, laboratory practicals, field excursions and research supervision across undergraduate, Master’s and PhD-level courses.





Contributed to laboratory practicals, field-based teaching and marine sampling exercises, introducing undergraduate students to Arctic marine ecosystems, biodiversity and ecological field methods.



Fieldwork views from 80°N on the bachelor course aboard R/V Helmer Hansen.


Less than a week worth of packing for the student cruise!

 Our sampling site was right infront of this!

 This device (CTD) measures Conductivity, Temperature and Density and a million other variables!






Contributed to lectures and practical teaching covering environmental DNA (eDNA), molecular ecology, metabarcoding, biodiversity assessment and bioinformatic approaches used to investigate Arctic marine ecosystems.



Helping hands!


What we sample for the course.


My very first teacher cabin aboard R/V Polarsyssel.


Students collecting environmental DNA samples.







A little break to watch a pod of belugas swimming through the fjord!





Contributed to teaching in Arctic microbial ecology, laboratory methods, microbial biodiversity, Arctic biogeochemistry and modern molecular and bioinformatic techniques used to study microorganisms in polar environments.








Students really digging into the pingo to hear the methane bubbles.




Prepping to start spring fieldwork


Cannot forget some marine fieldwork!





Alongside formal teaching, I have mentored and supervised undergraduate and Master’s students, as well as bachelor interns, in all field- lab- and computer-based research. Working closely with students on an individual basis has been one of the most rewarding aspects of teaching. It has provided the opportunity to tailor guidance to each student’s goals, help them develop practical research skills and build confidence as future scientists.
This has included guidance in:

Arctic field sampling
Molecular laboratory techniques
DNA metabarcoding workflows
Data preparation and analysis




Summer fieldwork!

title: My First Job Outside Academia
text:
After completing my PhD, I decided to step outside academia for a few months to see what life beyond research might look like. During the summer of 2025, I worked as a city guide in Longyearbyen, Svalbard, leading visitors through one of the world’s northernmost settlements.
My days were incredibly varied. One moment I was guiding walking tours through town, and the next I was flipping pancakes, serving reindeer soup, or welcoming guests while telling stories about Willem Barentsz, Arctic exploration, polar bears and life in the High Arctic.
Although the tours were primarily centred around the history of Longyearbyen, I naturally found myself talking about science instead. I shared stories from my five years living in Svalbard, my PhD research, the fascinating work of friends and colleagues, and what we are witnessing firsthand as residents of a rapidly changing Arctic.
To my surprise, these were often the conversations people remembered most. Visitors were genuinely curious about Arctic research and what scientists are discovering in one of the fastest-changing regions on Earth. Rather than pulling me away from academia, this experience reinforced how much I enjoy connecting people with science through storytelling.

title: Featured
text:
This page highlights news stories, media coverage and public features in which my research and outreach activities have been featured.



University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS)
Throughout my PhD at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS), my research and fieldwork were featured in several university news stories highlighting Arctic research, long-term ecological monitoring, education and field expeditions.

Featured stories

Stepping Back into the Past – UNIS Repeat a 30-Year-Old Beach Study in Storfjorden
Revisiting Arctic coastal ecosystems after more than 30 years to investigate how climate change has altered intertidal biodiversity.
👉 Read the article →



Fieldwork in the Time of Corona
Continuing long-term Arctic ecosystem monitoring during the COVID-19 pandemic while maintaining one of Svalbard’s longest-running marine time series.
👉 Read the article →





The Nansen Legacy
As part of The Nansen Legacy, one of Norway’s largest Arctic research programmes, I contributed to outreach activities that communicated Arctic microbial ecology and long-term ecosystem change to the wider public.

Featured stories

The Hunt for the Hidden Life in Water
Sharing my PhD research on microbial life in Arctic waters and explaining why microscopic organisms are essential for understanding ecosystem change.
👉 Read the article →





Science Communication & Education

UNISprout
Featured through UNISprout, an educational initiative that connects bachelor students with active Arctic research projects, highlighting my role in mentoring students during fieldwork and laboratory activities.
👉 Read the article →



Norwegian Polar Institute
Tiny Arctic Wildlife Matters
During a research cruise aboard R/V Kronprins Haakon, I wrote about life at sea, Arctic fieldwork and why microscopic organisms are essential for understanding Arctic ecosystems.
👉 Read the blog →




Documentary & Media
My research and life as an Arctic scientist were also featured in international media documenting climate change and scientific research in Svalbard.

Euronews × Denis Loctier
Polar Opposite: How Climate Change is Altering the Arctic
Featured in the Euronews documentary Polar Opposite, filmed in Svalbard, highlighting Arctic research and the impacts of climate change on polar ecosystems.
👉 Watch the documentary →

The documentary and behind-the-scenes production can also be viewed on Denis Loctier’s portfolio website.
👉 View the production page →

title: Science Through Poetry
text:
The ISA Time Series: A Musical
Written during my PhD in Svalbard as part of a science outreach event for the Longyearbyen community.
Once upon a time, in a land far, far away,  
In a place we all call India,  
lived a little girl that went by the name Cheshtaa.

She lived in a city of twelve million,  
all having the same vision.

When all her friends wanted to become  
doctors, engineers or lawyers,  
little Cheshtaa did not care about the dollars.

She wanted to study life  
in extreme habitats...  
or on another planet.

But there was no such study  
like that in India.

Damn it!

As you can see,  
she was exposed to marine biology.

No... she wasn't scared of boats  
or seasick—  
don't go by the chronology.

Despite her fears and her very sad survival skills,  
she moved to another continent, with only seven suitcases.

Maybe that was an overkill?!

She travelled through several countries  
on a student visa,  
discovering new places, new cultures,  
and many eateries.

She the travelled to several countries
on a student visa with multipe enteries, 
She was in places where they didnt speak what she spoke,
but nonehtless saw reindeers, yetis and famous statues despite being broke.

To find the answer to her questions,
she studied
studied  
and ahem studied, 

Until she GRADUATED!

Confused about where life would take her next, 
she decided to go for a PhD which could be complex..

At the age of 24, she was going to explore,
Organisms living in extreme habitats furthermore...

To her surprise, she had to analyse,
changes in PROTIST diversity, at the University!

Why were these little ones changing in numbers you may ask,
To find this out was infact her task!

She read and read and read and found, 
The Arctic was warming up Atlantic water all around

Could this be the reason they change in number?
From winter to winter and summer to summer?

She set sail on her boats every month with Audun, and Charlie and Edmund...

To collect and filter samples of water
And to keep in her conscience copepod slaughter
She also collected data for temperature from a CTD, 
And that of light and salinity all from the sea..


After filtering all the water for environmental DNA,
she sent off her samples to a land far far away (Canada)

In the meantime she got the opportunity to do other cool works,
I guess studying at UNIS has its own perks!


When she got the results what do you think she saw?
What I am about to tell you will make you drop your jaw!

It wasnt changing climate that was affecting these cuties,
It was more light and seasons that was changing the species.

If youre wondering, here is a figure of watermasses, 
its not just about how hot or cold it is, there are many different classes,
Dont you see the amount of warm Atlantic water we are getting through the years??
There are no trends shown in my data and that brings me to tears...

Do you see in 2014 the amount of red?? 
How organisms dont get affected by this just doesnt get in my head!

Everyone told her "Just write about seasonality.", 
No result is also a result you see!
She came up with the idea of only looking at data during the polar night, 
Surely that is affected by warm Atlantic water if theres no light?

BUT no again, cmon WHAT?!?
How can it still be seasonal despite all that?!

For 3 years she worked and worked and worked but did she find out??

You would just have to stay tuned to clear your doubt!




← Back to Outreach

title: Space Biology
text:
Space Biology
Although my current research focuses on sustainable agriculture on Earth, I am fascinated by how microbe–mineral interactions could help support future human exploration of space.


For me, space biology represents the intersection of nearly everything that has shaped my scientific journey—from microbial ecology and molecular biology to sustainable agriculture and environmental sequencing. It is a field where ecology, bioinformatics and human exploration converge, and one that I hope to contribute to in the future.



Background animation © European Space Agency (ESA)

title: Arctic Marine Ecology
text:
During my PhD at the University Centre in Svalbard (UNIS), I investigated how microbial eukaryotic communities respond to environmental variability across Arctic fjords. Using one of the longest environmental DNA time series from the European Arctic, this work explored seasonal succession, hydrographic influences and long-term community stability.





How do microbial eukaryotic communities vary across seasons and contrasting Arctic fjords?
What roles do light availability, hydrography and other environmental gradients play in shaping microbial biodiversity?
How stable are Arctic microbial communities over annual to decadal timescales in a rapidly changing climate?

title: Arctic Marine Ecology

title: Arctic Marine Ecology
section: Isfjorden: A Natural Laboratory for Arctic Microbial Ecology
text:
Isfjorden: A Natural Laboratory for Arctic Microbial Ecology
My PhD research was conducted within Isfjorden, which is the largest fjord system on the west coast of Svalbard and provides a natural laboratory for studying environmental variability.
Throughout my PhD, I investigated microbial eukaryotic communities across this fjord system using environmental DNA and long-term ecological observations. The project resulted in three first-author publications, each addressing complementary aspects of Arctic microbial ecology.





Map of Svalbard showing the major ocean currents influencing the archipelago. The red box highlights Isfjorden, the primary study area used throughout my PhD research.

title: Arctic Marine Ecology
text:
Paper 1 — Published

Chitkara et al. (2024)


Research summary
This study is a comparative synthesis of phytoplankton bloom dynamics across three Arctic fjords (Greenland, Norway, and Svalbard).


Key findings

Spring phytoplankton blooms followed remarkably similar seasonal patterns across Arctic fjords despite contrasting environmental conditions.
Summer and autumn blooms were controlled by local hydrographic processes, including glacier-driven upwelling, terrestrial runoff and wind mixing.
The study demonstrates that future climate change will influence Arctic primary production differently among fjords depending on their physical setting.

title: Arctic Marine Ecology
text:
Paper 2 — Submitted

Comparative microbial community dynamics across contrasting Arctic fjords
This manuscript compares microbial eukaryotic communities between two contrasting fjord systems within the Isfjorden on Svalbard. Based on a year-long monthly environmental time series, the study investigates how differences in hydrography, light availability and local environmental conditions shape seasonal and spatial patterns in microbial biodiversity.
(Currently under peer review.)

title: Arctic Marine Ecology
text:
Paper 3 — In Preparation
This work expands the understanding of Arctic microbial community dynamics over a period of 10 years in the high-Arctic
(Currently in preparation)

title: Benthic Ecology
text:
Paper 1 — Published

Sen et al. (2019)


Research summary
This study characterized a newly discovered Arctic methane cold seep using ROV imagery, GIS mapping, sediment geochemistry and benthic community analyses.


Key findings

Characterized a previously unknown Arctic methane cold seep ecosystem.
Recorded the first evidence of frenulate siboglinid worms colonizing hard carbonate substrates.
Identified the seep as a probable nursery ground for Arctic skate egg cases.
Demonstrated how sulphide-rich seep environments structure Arctic benthic communities.

title: Benthic Ecology
text:
Paper 2 — Published

Sen et al. (2019)


Research summary
This study compared benthic communities at an Arctic methane seep with the surrounding background seafloor using high-resolution image mosaics.


Key findings

Weak hydrocarbon seepage significantly increased megabenthic diversity and abundance.
Carbonate crusts acted as biodiversity hotspots by providing habitat for benthic organisms.
Ecological effects of seep habitats persist even after seep activity declines.
The findings have important implications for Arctic marine conservation and offshore resource management.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text: I am a molecular ecologist with expertise in Arctic microbial ecology, environmental DNA, molecular biology and bioinformatics. My research combines long-term ecological monitoring, high-throughput sequencing and computational analyses to investigate microbial communities across Arctic and terrestrial ecosystems.
